timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Call of Jeremiah

sections.header: Verses 1–13 — "They Followed Worthless Idols and Became Worthless"

heb.paragraph: God remembers Israel's youthful ḥesed — the covenant loyalty of the wilderness period when the nation followed God through the desert "like a bride." This tender recollection makes the subsequent indictment more devastating: the bride has become a harlot, abandoning the ḥesed that defined the covenant relationship.

Israel "followed worthless idols (hebel) and became worthless (hebel) themselves." The same word Ecclesiastes uses for the futility of life here describes the spiritual entropy of idolatry — worshippers become like what they worship. The pun is biting: pursue nothing, become nothing.

refs.note: Hosea similarly recalls the wilderness period as a time of intimate devotion between God and Israel — the honeymoon before the unfaithfulness.
Jesus identifies himself as the source of "living water" — the very thing Judah abandoned in Jeremiah 2:13. The image moves from indictment to invitation.
"Those who make idols become like them" — the same principle Jeremiah articulates with the hebel wordplay.

notes.note: God recalls Israel's early devotion with tenderness. Israel was "holy to the LORD, the firstfruits of his harvest." This is covenant language — Israel's special status made attacking her a sacrilege, like consuming consecrated grain.

Four categories of leaders failed: priests did not seek God, those who handled the law did not know God, shepherds (rulers) rebelled, prophets prophesied by Baal. Comprehensive leadership failure at every level.
"Has a nation ever changed its gods? (Yet they are not gods at all.)" Even pagan nations showed more loyalty to their false deities than Israel showed to the true God — a devastating comparison.
Two evils in one act: forsaking the fountain of living water and digging broken cisterns. The metaphor is particularly powerful in arid Palestine, where water was life itself.

Calvin notes that God does not recall Israel's wilderness devotion because it was perfect, but because it was sincere relative to the current apostasy. Even imperfect faith can reflect genuine covenant loyalty.

Calvin calls this "one of the most memorable sentences in all of Scripture." The double indictment reveals that apostasy is never merely negative (leaving God) but always positive (replacing God with something inferior). Humans cannot sustain a vacuum — they will worship something.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Hebrew kǎllâ ("bride") is used here metaphorically for Israel's early relationship with God. The marriage metaphor for the covenant relationship is characteristic of both Jeremiah and Hosea.
The phrase "those who deal with the law" (tōpĕśê hattôrâ) likely refers to the priests whose duty was to teach and interpret Torah, not to scribes or legal professionals.
Cisterns in Palestine were typically hewn from limestone and plastered to hold rainwater. Cracked cisterns would lose their water — a metaphor for the futility of trusting in anything other than God.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies vv.1–13 as a self-contained oracle structured as a covenant lawsuit (rîb). The unit moves from tender recollection (vv.1–3) through accusation (vv.4–8) to formal indictment (vv.9–13). The "two evils" of v.13 serve as the climactic charge, summarizing all of Israel's failures in one devastating image.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann observes that the indictment targets every mediating institution — priests, Torah handlers, rulers, prophets. The failure is systemic, not individual. When the entire knowledge-producing apparatus of a society serves false narratives, truth becomes inaccessible to the ordinary person.

hist.context: Chapter 2 opens the great covenant lawsuit (rîb) that runs through chapters 2–6. God appears as both the aggrieved husband and the prosecuting attorney. The literary form draws on ancient Near Eastern treaty-violation lawsuits: recollection of past benefits (vv.1–3), accusation of breach (vv.4–8), formal charges (vv.9–13). Verse 13 contains one of the most powerful images in the Hebrew Bible: Israel has committed two evils — forsaking the spring of living water and hewing out cracked cisterns that cannot hold water.

sections.header: Verses 14–25 — "You Have Brought This on Yourself"

heb.paragraph: Jeremiah compares Israel to a wild donkey in heat (v.24) — driven by insatiable desire, impossible to restrain. The image is deliberately crude and shocking, stripping away any pretense of dignity in Israel's pursuit of foreign gods. The animal metaphor exposes idolatry as compulsive, irrational appetite.

refs.note: Isaiah's Song of the Vineyard uses the same image of a cultivated vine producing only bad fruit — a parallel indictment of Israel's moral degeneration.
Paul's description of humanity exchanging the glory of God for images follows the same theological logic: abandoning the Creator leads to degrading worship.

notes.note: Israel was not born a slave, yet she has become one by choice. Memphis (Noph) and Tahpanhes represent Egypt's power — the very bondage God had freed Israel from. Returning to Egypt is a reversal of the Exodus.
The vine metaphor: God planted a choice vine (śōrēq), but it degenerated into a wild vine. No amount of ritual purification ("soap and lye") can remove the stain of deep-rooted spiritual adultery.

The camel and wild donkey metaphors portray Israel's idolatry as an animal-like compulsion. "In her heat" is explicit sexual language applied to spiritual unfaithfulness — a rhetorical strategy designed to shock the audience into self-recognition.

Calvin argues that divine punishment is not arbitrary but organic: "Your own wickedness will punish you." Sin carries its own consequences. God does not need to impose punishment externally — the natural fruit of apostasy is ruin.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Memphis (Noph) and Tahpanhes were major Egyptian cities. The reference to "shaving the crown of your head" may refer to Egyptian domination or to a humiliating defeat.

The verb translated "broke your yoke" should be read as first person: "I broke your yoke." God freed Israel from slavery, but Israel responded by spreading herself under every green tree — a reference to Canaanite fertility cult worship.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies this as a series of rhetorical questions building toward an irrefutable conclusion. Each question expects the answer "no," forcing the audience to confront the absurdity of their behavior. The effect is cumulative — by v.25, Israel's self-defense has been systematically demolished.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann notes that the sexual metaphors are not merely rhetorical flourishes but reflect Jeremiah's understanding of idolatry as a form of addiction — a compulsion that strips away rational agency and reduces the worshipper to instinctual behavior.

hist.context: The metaphors intensify: Israel is a slave (v.14), a vine gone wild (v.21), a stained garment that no soap can clean (v.22), a wild donkey in heat (v.24). Each image strips away another layer of national self-deception. The references to Egypt and Assyria (vv.18, 36) reflect Judah's vacillating foreign policy — seeking alliances with great powers rather than trusting in God.

sections.header: Verses 26–37 — "Where Are the Gods You Made for Yourself?"

heb.paragraph: The root bôš ("shame") dominates vv.26–36, appearing in multiple forms. The thief is ashamed when caught — so Israel will be ashamed when their idols fail. But the shame has not yet arrived; the present tense shows Israel still brazenly saying "I am innocent" (v.35). The gap between deserved shame and actual shamelessness is the heart of the indictment.

refs.note: Elijah's mockery of Baal on Mount Carmel — "Perhaps he is sleeping" — embodies the same challenge: where are your gods when you need them?
Moses' Song anticipates the same taunt: "Where are their gods, the rock they took refuge in?" — the covenant lawsuit form is deeply rooted in Deuteronomic tradition.

notes.note: The charge of saying to wood "You are my father" and to stone "You gave me birth" is a bitter parody of genuine worship. Israel has replaced the Creator with created objects and attributed generative power to lifeless materials.

The blood of the innocent on Israel's garments refers to social injustice accompanying religious apostasy — idolatry and injustice are always linked in prophetic theology. Yet Israel claims innocence, compounding guilt with self-deception.

Calvin observes that the multiplicity of gods reveals the insecurity of idolatry: one god is never enough because no idol can actually deliver. The proliferation of shrines is evidence not of piety but of desperation.

Calvin is especially severe on Israel's claim of innocence: "This is the worst evil — when sinners, being caught in their sin, still dare to deny their guilt." Self-justification in the face of clear evidence is the final barrier to repentance.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Hebrew is literally "saying to a tree, 'You are my father,' and to a stone, 'You gave birth to me.'" This may reflect Asherah pole worship (wood) and standing stone worship (massebah) — both elements of Canaanite cult practice.
The phrase "how skilled you are at pursuing love" uses the verb yāṭab ("to make good/skillful") with cutting irony — Israel has perfected the art of unfaithfulness.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom structures this as the peroration of the covenant lawsuit — the concluding argument after evidence has been presented. The section builds to the devastating verdict in v.37: "The LORD has rejected those you trust; you will not be helped by them." The lawsuit ends without acquittal.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann identifies Israel's claim "I am innocent" as the paradigmatic form of false consciousness. The capacity to deny one's own guilt in the face of overwhelming evidence is not merely a moral failing but a cognitive distortion produced by ideological self-interest.

hist.context: The chapter closes with a courtroom scene: Israel pleads innocent (v.35), but God brings charges. The challenge "Where are your gods?" (v.28) exposes the fundamental bankruptcy of idolatry — when crisis comes, the gods Israel chose over the LORD are nowhere to be found. The reference to "as many gods as you have towns" (v.28) suggests the proliferation of local cults throughout Judah.

themes.note: Covenant and Judgment both peak at 9 — this is a covenant lawsuit prosecuting Israel's breach. Worship at 8 reflects the chapter's pervasive concern with idolatry versus true worship.

lit.note: The chapter follows the form of an ancient Near Eastern covenant lawsuit (rîb): past benefits recalled, charges brought, evidence presented, verdict delivered. Verse 13 is the structural hinge — the "two evils" that summarize all subsequent indictments.

hebtext.note: God remembers Israel's youthful ḥesed — the covenant loyalty of the wilderness period when the nation followed God through the desert "like a bride." This tender recollection makes the subsequent indictment more devastating: the bride has become a harlot, abandoning the ḥesed that defined the covenant relationship.

Israel "followed worthless idols (hebel) and became worthless (hebel) themselves." The same word Ecclesiastes uses for the futility of life here describes the spiritual entropy of idolatry — worshippers become like what they worship. The pun is biting: pursue nothing, become nothing.

Jeremiah compares Israel to a wild donkey in heat (v.24) — driven by insatiable desire, impossible to restrain. The image is deliberately crude and shocking, stripping away any pretense of dignity in Israel's pursuit of foreign gods. The animal metaphor exposes idolatry as compulsive, irrational appetite.

The root bôš ("shame") dominates vv.26–36, appearing in multiple forms. The thief is ashamed when caught — so Israel will be ashamed when their idols fail. But the shame has not yet arrived; the present tense shows Israel still brazenly saying "I am innocent" (v.35). The gap between deserved shame and actual shamelessness is the heart of the indictment.

ppl.name: Jeremiah
ppl.text: The marriage metaphor for the covenant relationship is characteristic of both Jeremiah and Hosea…

translations.text: This is what the Lord says: “What fault did your ancestors find in me, that they strayed so far from me? They followed worthless idols and became worthless themselves.
Thus says the LORD: “What wrong did your fathers find in me that they went far from me, and went after worthlessness, and became worthless?

“Go and proclaim in the hearing of Jerusalem: “This is what the Lord says: “ ‘I remember the devotion of your youth, how as a bride you loved me and followed me through the wilderness, through a land not sown.
“Go and proclaim in the hearing of Jerusalem, Thus says the LORD, “I remember the devotion of your youth, your love as a bride, how you followed me in the wilderness, in a land not sown.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "God recalls Israel's early devotion with tenderness. Israel was "holy to the LORD, the firstfruits of his harvest." This is covenant language — Israel's special status made attacking her a sacrilege, …"

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin notes that God does not recall Israel's wilderness devotion because it was perfect, but because it was sincere relative to the current apostasy. Even imperfect faith can reflect genuine covenan…"

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The Hebrew kǎllâ ("bride") is used here metaphorically for Israel's early relationship with God. The marriage metaphor for the covenant relationship is characteristic of both Jeremiah and Hosea."

debate: The phrase "those who deal with the law" (tōpĕśê hattôrâ) likely refers to the priests whose duty was to teach and interpret Torah, not to scribes or …
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Israel's Apostasy within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: Hosea similarly recalls the wilderness period as a time of intimate devotion between God and Israel — the honeymoon before the unfaithfulness.
Jesus identifies himself as the source of "living water" — the very thing Judah abandoned in Jeremiah 2:13. The image moves from indictment to invitation.
"Those who make idols become like them" — the same principle Jeremiah articulates with the hebel wordplay.
Isaiah's Song of the Vineyard uses the same image of a cultivated vine producing only bad fruit — a parallel indictment of Israel's moral degeneration.
Paul's description of humanity exchanging the glory of God for images follows the same theological logic: abandoning the Creator leads to degrading worship.
Elijah's mockery of Baal on Mount Carmel — "Perhaps he is sleeping" — embodies the same challenge: where are your gods when you need them?

coaching.tip: 'My people have committed two sins: They have forsaken me, the spring of living water, and have dug their own cisterns, broken cisterns that cannot hold water' (v. 13). Jeremiah reduces all of Israel's failures to one image: trading a spring for a cracked jar. The sin isn't just choosing wrongly — it's choosing something that can't even deliver what it promises. Idolatry doesn't fail morally first; it fails practically.

The entire chapter is a covenant lawsuit — God as plaintiff, Israel as defendant. But the charges aren't abstract sins; they're relational betrayals. God recalls the 'love of your youth' (v. 2) like a jilted spouse reading old love letters. Jeremiah's God is not a judge delivering a verdict from above but a wounded partner testifying from within the relationship.
